desc: creates a new branch and switches to it
deletes a local branch
undoes the last commit but keeps the changes
temporarily saves uncommitted changes
pushes a new branch to remote and tracks it
lists all containers, running and stopped
force removes a container
opens a shell inside a running container
makes a file executable
force stops a process
scans open ports and detects service versions
scans a web server for known vulnerabilities
finds sql injection points and lists databases
sends icmp echo requests to check if a host is alive
ping sweep scan, checks if the host is up without port scanning
arp ping scan, discovers hosts on the local network using arp
echo (icmp) ping scan, forces icmp instead of arp for host discovery
list scan, lists targets and does reverse dns lookup without sending any packets
standard scan on local network, uses arp ping and scans default 1000 ports
insane timing, fast scan of the 100 most common ports
fast scan of an entire subnet for open web service ports, skips host discovery
scans a single specific port
syn stealth scan, half-open scan that doesn't complete the tcp handshake
tcp connect scan, completes the full tcp handshake (noisier than syn scan)
fin scan, sends a fin flag packet to try to slip past simple firewalls
xmas scan, sends fin, psh, and urg flags together to evade basic firewalls
null scan, sends a packet with no flags set to evade basic packet filters
ack scan, used to map out firewall rulesets rather than find open ports
window scan, similar to ack scan but examines the tcp window field to guess port state
maimon scan, sends fin/ack packets, useful against certain bsd-derived systems
fragments packets into smaller pieces to bypass some firewalls and ids
spoofs the source ip address of the scan (needs the correct network interface)
idle scan, uses a third-party zombie host to scan a target anonymously
sends packets with an intentionally invalid checksum to test how a firewall reacts
attempts an ftp bounce scan through a vulnerable ftp server as a proxy
scans udp ports instead of tcp
sctp init scan, similar to a syn scan but for the sctp protocol
scans which ip protocols (tcp, udp, icmp, etc) are supported by the host
attempts to detect the operating system of the target
aggressive scan, enables os detection, version detection, script scanning, and traceroute together
detects service versions and runs default nse scripts, reveals details like rpc services
shows the network hops between you and the target
traces the route to the target and attempts to geolocate each hop
performs a whois lookup on the target ip
crawls a web server and generates a directory/sitemap structure

attempts a dns zone transfer to dump all dns records
checks a dns server's cache for previously resolved domains

runs all nse vulnerability detection scripts against the target
runs vulnerability scripts against a specific set of ports only

extracts the password hash from a zip file so john can crack it
extracts the password hash from a rar archive so john can crack it
extracts the passphrase hash from an ssh private key so john can crack it
extracts the password hash from a protected pdf file so john can crack it
extracts the password hash from a password protected word or excel file so john can crack it
combines the passwd and shadow files into one file that john can crack
keywords: prepare linux password file for cracking

desc: cracks a password hash using john's default mode order (single crack, then wordlist, then incremental)
cracks a password hash using a wordlist, also known as a dictionary attack
keywords: unlock the file using a wordlist

desc: runs a dictionary attack with word-mangling rules applied to each wordlist entry, e.g. adding numbers or symbols
tries every possible character combination, a true brute-force attack (slow but thorough)
cracks a password using a defined pattern, for example four digits only
tries passwords derived from the username and account information, fast and a good first attempt
manually specifies the hash type when john can't auto-detect it
displays passwords that have already been cracked
cracks the password for one specific user only
names the current cracking session so it can be paused and resumed later
resumes a previously paused cracking session
splits the cracking job across multiple cpu cores to speed it up
benchmarks john's cracking speed on the current machine
lists every hash format that john supports
